does: This paper proposes Staged-Competence, a curriculum learning framework that enhances safety alignment in large language models trained with Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO). The approach organises preference data by difficulty level, uses competence-based sampling to select training examples matched to the model's current capabilities, and progressively updates the reference model throughout training. Evaluated across three model families, the method achieves a 16% reduction in out-of-distribution harmful response rates and 20% reduction in successful jailbreak attacks compared to standard DPO, while maintaining general model capabilities and avoiding over-refusal. The framework additionally demonstrates data efficiency by matching baseline safety performance with only 75% of the original training data and produces clearer separation between safe and unsafe response distributions.
connects: This work builds on the extensive literature on Direct Preference Optimisation for alignment, responding to demonstrated brittleness and poor out-of-distribution generalisation in prior DPO-based safety approaches. It applies curriculum learning principles—well-established in supervised learning for improving sample efficiency and robustness—to the preference optimisation setting. The paper connects to research on data ordering and progressive training in machine learning, as well as alignment work exploring how training procedures and data composition affect safety properties. It extends beyond prior work that identifies DPO's vulnerabilities to distribution shift by offering a structured methodology to address these issues, drawing on insights that models learn more robustly when examples are presented in order of increasing difficulty.
enables: This work opens pathways for more robust safety alignment practices by establishing curriculum learning as a general principle applicable across optimisation objectives. The framework's agnosticism to the specific policy loss function suggests immediate extensions to other DPO variants and alternative alignment methods. The demonstrated data efficiency gains enable training safety-aligned models with reduced computational and annotation costs. Future work could explore optimal difficulty metrics for preference data, extend the approach to multi-objective alignment combining safety with other desirable properties, or investigate how curriculum strategies interact with different model scales and architectures. The clearer decision boundary between safe and unsafe responses hints at improved interpretability of safety mechanisms, potentially enabling better understanding of what makes alignment robust.
